title: LC ETS Flavor Model -- Listen to a Conversation

words_per_turn.note: Varies: short turns (1-5 words) for reactions, long turns (30-50 words) for explanations

conversation_design.structure_pattern: 1) Opening/problem presentation -> 2) Information exchange/discussion -> 3) Resolution or action plan
conversation_design.structure_note: 73% of samples follow problem-discussion-resolution. The first 1-2 turns establish the situation, middle turns exchange information, final turns resolve with an action plan.

student_student.description: Two students discussing a campus situation (friends, classmates, roommates)

student_staff.description: Student interacting with campus staff (librarian, office worker, dining staff, IT)

role_pool.note: Staff roles should be campus service workers, NOT professors (professors are for lecture-type questions)

speaker_design.name_assignment: Each speaker gets a first name. Use common English names. Avoid ambiguous gender-neutral names when TTS voice assignment matters.

voice_assignment.student_staff: student voice + authority voice (librarian, advisor presets)

contractions.note: 91% of conversations use contractions. Mandatory for natural sound.

exclamations.note: Short emotional reactions add naturalness: 'Not again!', 'That sounds perfect!', 'Good call.'

dialogue_register.questions_between_speakers: Speakers naturally ask each other questions. Average 2-3 questions per conversation.
register_rules: Use contractions freely (don't, I'm, it's, they'll, won't)
Include 1-2 discourse markers per conversation (Actually, Well, Oh, Hmm)
Use casual vocabulary (sounds great, good call, yikes, no worries)
Avoid formal/academic vocabulary in student-student conversations
Staff speech can be slightly more formal but still conversational

main_idea.description: Why did speaker approach/what is conversation mainly about

inference.description: What will speaker likely do next / what can be inferred

q1_vs_q2_pattern.Q1: Usually detail (45%) or main_idea (55%) -- establishes the situation/problem
q1_vs_q2_pattern.Q2: Usually inference (73%) or detail (27%) -- tests what happens next or deeper understanding
q1_vs_q2_pattern.rule: Q1 tests WHAT is happening. Q2 tests WHAT COMES NEXT or WHY.

main_idea: What is the conversation mainly about?
detail: What does the {speaker} think should change?
inference: What will the {speaker} most likely do next?
What will the {speaker} likely do differently next time?
What is an advantage of the new system?

option_design.WARNING: 50% correct-is-longest in reference data is a bias to avoid. Target: <=30%.
option_design.WARNING_2: Severe B-clustering and D-absence. Generator MUST enforce even A/B/C/D distribution.

for_detail_questions.description: Uses words/concepts from conversation but changes a key fact
for_detail_questions.example: The shop will call her (wrong: they'll text)

for_detail_questions.description: Plausible action but NOT mentioned in conversation
for_detail_questions.example: Print the paper at home instead (never mentioned)

for_detail_questions.description: Takes a detail and attributes it to wrong speaker/context
for_detail_questions.example: The woman will fix the printer (wrong: she warned against it)

for_main_idea_questions.description: Plausible campus topic but not THIS conversation
for_main_idea_questions.example: To report a stolen bicycle (he came about a warning sticker)

for_main_idea_questions.description: Addresses one detail but misses the overall purpose
for_main_idea_questions.example: To ask about campus shuttle routes (too specific)
To extend her reservation (she's resolving a conflict)

for_inference_questions.description: Goes too far beyond what's stated
for_inference_questions.example: Complain to the university president (too extreme)

for_inference_questions.description: Something the speaker considered but decided against
for_inference_questions.example: Go to the student union building (he chose NOT to do this)

for_inference_questions.description: Plausible action but not connected to conversation
for_inference_questions.example: Ask a friend for a ride (not relevant to the discussion)

easy.conversation: Short (80-120 words), 6-8 turns, clear problem and resolution
easy.Q1: detail -- answer is directly stated in conversation

medium.conversation: Medium (120-160 words), 8-10 turns, information exchange with some back-and-forth
medium.Q1: main_idea or detail -- requires understanding the overall situation
medium.Q2: inference -- what will happen next based on what was discussed
medium.distractors: Plausible but use wrong details from conversation

hard.conversation: Long (160-200 words), 10-14 turns, multiple topics or a twist/complication
hard.Q1: main_idea -- purpose is not obvious from first turn
hard.Q2: inference -- requires connecting multiple pieces of information
hard.distractors: Very plausible, use conversation vocabulary, rejected alternatives as traps

flavor_markers.target: Contractions, fillers, discourse markers present. Sounds like real speech.
Each speaker has 3+ turns and contributes meaningfully to the conversation.
Q1 and Q2 test different skills (detail vs inference, main_idea vs detail).
All distractors are plausible campus scenarios. No absurd or obviously wrong options.
Correct answer is longest <=30% of time.

anti_patterns: Conversation is too short (<80 words) -- not enough material for 2 questions
Only one speaker talks -- both must participate meaningfully
Conversation sounds scripted/formal -- must use casual spoken register
No contractions used -- every LC conversation should have contractions
Both questions test the exact same piece of information
Correct answer for both Q1 and Q2 is the same letter
A distractor repeats conversation text verbatim
Questions can be answered using common sense alone (no listening needed)
Speaker names are inconsistent between speakers array and conversation turns
Conversation lacks a clear topic or purpose -- feels like random exchanges
All turns are the same length -- real conversations have short reactions and longer explanations

student_student.note: Use different gendered voices for clarity

student_staff.note: Staff voices should sound slightly more authoritative
